straight_beat.name: Chet Pazulski
straight_beat.column:
The Pirates’ 16-5 loss to the Nationals yesterday wasn’t just another blowout—it was a masterclass in how a team with talent can still look like a mess. Let’s dissect this like a cold brew, slow and deliberate.  

First, the pitching. Skenes, the kid with the 98 mph fastball, struck out

optimist.name: Franny Harsanyi
optimist.column:
The Pirates lost yesterday, but let’s not dwell on that. Let’s talk about Paul Skenes. That kid’s got something special. I’ve been saying it since he first stepped on the mound, and I’m not done yet. He’s not just a pitcher—he’s a lightning rod for hope. When he took the hill yesterday, I could feel the energy in the stadium. Not the kind that comes from a win, but the kind that says, “This is just the beginning.”  

Skenes threw 102 pitches, and I almost called my daughter to come over and watch. That’s the feeling that comes back. That’s why we keep showing up. You don’t throw that many pitches unless you’re fighting for every inch. You don’t throw that many pitches unless you believe in the script. Steve Blass used to say, “You’ve got to believe in the game,” and Smoky Burgess would’ve told you the same. Paul’s got that in his blood.  

Now, I know the record’s 10-6, and the math says we’re three games back in the Central. But here’s the thing: this team’s got legs. They’ve got young arms, young hearts, and a coach who’s got a plan. You can’t measure progress in wins and losses right now. You measure it in moments like yesterday’s game, when the Pirates came out swinging, when the offense showed flashes of brilliance, and when the defense made plays that’ll save games down the line.  

I’ll admit it’s frustrating to watch a team that’s supposed to be building stumble into a loss. But frustration’s just a temporary emotion. Optimism’s a choice. And I choose to believe in this team. They’ve got the kind of talent that

pessimist.name: Wally Horvath
pessimist.column:
The Pirates handed Washington their second straight shutout, and for once, it wasn’t because the Nats were bad. No, this was the kind of performance that makes you want to rip your hair out. A 16-5 thrashing? That’s not just a loss—it’s a statement. A declaration that the Pirates are still stuck in the same place they’ve been for decades: a team that can’t win, can’t adjust, and can’t stop wasting talent.  

The record’s 10-6, but that’s not the point. The point is the gap. Four games behind the Cardinals? That’s not a problem—it’s a indictment. You don’t build a contender by sitting around waiting for the weather to clear. You trade for pitchers, you draft smarter, you don’t let your best players walk because you’re too busy arguing with your own front office. But that’s exactly what’s happening. Skenes pitches the game of his life and Nutting’s in a luxury box counting peanuts. The Pirates are a tax write-off with a roster attached.  

Look, I get that rebuilding takes time. But time is a luxury you don’t have when you’re already four games back. The NL Central isn’t getting easier, and the Mets and Cardinals are figuring that out every day. Meanwhile, the Pirates are still stuck in 2015, trying to make sense of a roster that looks like it was assembled by a drunk Scrabble player. There’s a reason why the team’s young stars are leaving—because they’re not being given the tools to succeed. You don’t let a 23-year-old shortstop walk because you’re too busy negotiating with a minor league pitcher. You don’t let a 25-year-old outfielder leave because you’re too proud to trade him for a prospect. You do what’s necessary.  

And that’s where Bob Nutting
